records.ety: [F. concerter, It. concertare, conertare, prob. from L. consertus, p. p. of conserere to join together; con- + serere to join together, influenced by concertare to contend; con- + centare to strive; properly, to try to decide; fr. cernere to distinguish. See Series, and cf. Concern.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Concerted; p. pr. & vb. n. Concerting.]

defs: To plan together; to settle or adjust by conference, agreement, or consultation.
quotes: It was concerted to begin the siege in March.

defs: To plan; to devise; to arrange.
quotes: A commander had more trouble to concert his defense before the people than to plan . . . the campaign.

defs: To act in harmony or conjunction; to form combined plans.
quotes: The ministers of Denmark were appointed to concert with Talbot.

records.ety: [F. concert, It. concerto, conserto, fr. concertare. See Concert, v. t.]

defs: Agreement in a design or plan; union formed by mutual communication of opinions and views; accordance in a scheme; harmony; simultaneous action.
quotes: All these discontents, how ruinous soever, have arisen from the want of a due communication and concert.
Let us in concert to the season sing.

defs: A musical entertainment in which several voices or instruments take part.
quotes: Visit by night your lady's chamber window With some sweet concert.
And boding screech owls make the concert full.
